nodes.label: Clients do not see MedBrief internal folders, only other client folders

provenance.quote: Clients don't see internal folders. So they don't see any of the MedBrief internal folders. They only see other client folders.

nodes.statement: Clients cannot see any MedBrief internal folders on the Records page; they only see other client folders.

nodes.label: A personal folder is created in both Case Documentation and Private Folders for every user

provenance.quote: whenever there's a private folder created in case documentation, the same folder is also created in private folders.

nodes.statement: Creating a personal folder in Case Documentation automatically creates a corresponding folder in Private Folders.

nodes.label: Private Folders for non-expert users may be obsolete

provenance.quote: the only folders that really get used in private folders are the experts. So I don't understand why everyone else is also in private folders. So it might just be a little bit obsolete.

nodes.statement: Chantel believes Private Folders are only meaningfully used for experts and the duplication for all other users may be obsolete legacy behaviour.

nodes.label: Client uploads to Unsorted Records cause duplication: ops must re-upload to the batch

provenance.quote: it creates a bit of duplication because if it is a matter that requires a sorting session, they, and the clients has uploaded their own records, they uploaded to this folder and then the ops team, they need to go download whatever the clients uploaded from this folder, go back to the Matter dashboard
they need to go and they need to upload the same folders that were uploaded here into the batch.

nodes.statement: When clients upload records to the Unsorted Records folder and a sorting session is required, the ops team must manually re-download and re-upload those records into the batch, causing duplicated effort.

nodes.label: Apryse was previously named PDFTron; it is the same viewer

provenance.quote: PDF Tron. Yeah, yeah, yeah. Is that what it, okay.
So apparently it's still the same viewer, just different name.

nodes.statement: The records viewer tool Apryse was previously called PDFTron; the underlying viewer technology is the same, only the name changed.

nodes.label: CER documents can range from 50 to 23,000 pages

provenance.quote: Those very long documents that can be very long from, from 50 pages up to like 23,000 pages.

nodes.statement: Computerised electronic record documents handled by MedBrief can range from 50 pages up to approximately 23,000 pages.

nodes.label: Epic CER bookmarks follow heading hierarchy (first, second, third, fourth level)

provenance.quote: This is the epic type CR. And then you get Oracle and you get a few others, but the epic ones are usually if they are bookmarked, they are bookmarked according to the headings.
So you'll, you'll get your first level heading, second level heading, second level heading. These are obviously third, second, third, third, fourth, et cetera.

nodes.statement: Epic-type CER documents are bookmarked according to their heading hierarchy (first through fourth level headings), though not every heading level is always bookmarked.

nodes.label: MedBrief does not classify or date sections within CER documents

provenance.quote: any computerized electronic records, when it goes into docs sort of, we don't do anything with it. We just classify it as a computerized electronic record. We do not date it. We do not even try to classify the sections within the documents at all.

nodes.statement: MedBrief's DocSorter process classifies CER documents as a whole type but does not date them or classify sections within them, because multiple clinical sections can appear on a single page.

nodes.label: Annotations are lost if a sorted set of records is subsequently updated

provenance.quote: with annotations, the moment a client applies annotations, especially to a sorted set of records, if that sorted set of records get updated, meaning it needs to go back to docs or they need to insert more pages because the trust or hospital sent us more pages for whatever reason, then those annotations that they make is lost.

nodes.statement: Any annotations a client applies to a sorted set of records are permanently lost if the records need to be updated (e.g., new pages inserted).

nodes.label: Redaction would also be lost on record update, and it is not yet implemented

provenance.quote: it's not something that has been implemented yet, but if we do implement it, it would be a reduction. Once we implement reduction, reduction would also be lost if the sorted records get updated.

nodes.statement: Redaction has not yet been implemented. If it is implemented, it will face the same loss-on-update problem as annotations.

nodes.label: Annotation visibility can be scoped to self, firm, or no one

provenance.quote: the user can add annotations. They can also choose who the annotation should be visible to. Should it be visible to no one or to just themselves or to everybody in their firm?

nodes.statement: Users can control the visibility of annotations: private (no one), self-only, or all members of their firm.

nodes.label: Ask Alex can only interrogate MedBrief-sorted records within the records parent folder

provenance.quote: they'd only be able to interrogate MedWeb sorted records. So in other words, it would have to be sorted records that's within the records parent folder.

nodes.statement: Ask Alex will only be able to query records that have been sorted by MedBrief and are within the records parent folder; it will not query unsorted or external records.

nodes.label: Ask Alex will display example/popular queries and allow file or folder scoping

provenance.quote: we do have examples or we will display examples of popular questions.
the moment a user selects a file or a folder, it will automatically apply that file or folder as a filter.

nodes.statement: Ask Alex will display pre-populated example queries and automatically apply a selected file or folder as a context filter for the AI query.

nodes.label: The MedBrief Insights chat interface was built from scratch in-house

provenance.quote: We built it from scratch.

nodes.statement: The MedBrief Insights chat interface was built in-house from scratch, not sourced from a third-party library.

nodes.label: MedBrief Insights / Ask Alex is not yet live; it is currently in development

provenance.quote: this is currently in development.
So is the whole MedBrief Insights panel there not live yet?

nodes.statement: The entire MedBrief Insights panel, including Ask Alex, is currently in development and not yet live.

nodes.label: MedBrief Insights will hyperlink AI answer references directly to the source documents

provenance.quote: anything that is referenced, any references found will hyperlink to the actual documents if they select it.

nodes.statement: When MedBrief Insights returns an answer, any referenced documents will be hyperlinked so the user can navigate directly to the source.

nodes.label: MedBrief Insights will include a feedback mechanism (thumbs up / down) for the AI team

provenance.quote: there's a feedback mechanism for the AI team to improve the model so the user can either say it's good or it's bad.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Insights will include a user feedback mechanism (good / bad rating) to help the AI team improve model quality.

nodes.label: CER index extracts bookmarks; falls back to VLM heading extraction if no bookmarks

provenance.quote: if it's got the bookmarks, then we're going to extract the bookmarks. If it doesn't, then we need to, they're using a visual language model, a BLM, to extract the headings.

nodes.statement: The CER index extracts bookmarks where they exist; if not, it uses a visual language model (VLM) to extract headings.

nodes.label: Identifying clinical sections by content is very difficult because content varies and multiple sections can appear on one page

provenance.quote: that is very difficult because the content is different...you can have four clinical sections on one page

nodes.statement: Automatically identifying clinical sections by content is very difficult because content varies across records and multiple clinical sections can appear on a single page.

nodes.label: CER index will be interactive: date-range filter and keyword search over headings

provenance.quote: The difference is that it's going to be interactive.
they're going to be able to filter it according to a date range
The other thing is there will be a keyword search.

nodes.statement: The new CER index will be interactive, supporting date-range filtering and keyword search over extracted headings.

nodes.label: Keyword search operates over heading content, not the entire document

provenance.quote: It's not searching the entire document. It's searching the content of the headings that's listed here.

nodes.statement: The keyword search in the CER index searches only the content of extracted headings, not the full document.

nodes.label: Patient section headings cannot be dated; only event-section headings carry dates

provenance.quote: the patient, the first section, the patient section is a bit hard to date because a lot of the time it's only a summary of stuff that's further down in the document
demographics, patient contacts, problem lists, all of that, it can't really be dated, or like allergies can't really be dated

nodes.statement: The patient section headings (demographics, contacts, problem lists, allergies) cannot be dated; only event-section headings receive date attribution.

nodes.label: Date extraction rules differ by event type: day visit returns a single date, admission returns a date range

provenance.quote: if it's an admission, we need to return a date range. If it's only a day event, we need to return it, just the event for that, or the date for that particular event.

nodes.statement: Day-visit events return a single date; admission events return a date range.

nodes.label: Date extraction is very tricky for sections with content-embedded dates such as 'Orders only'

provenance.quote: it's very, very tricky to do because depending on the content, there's different types of dates that you need to look for
there's different rules for different things, for different contents. So it is very, very tricky. It does make it difficult for the AI team.

nodes.statement: Extracting dates from sections with content-embedded dates (such as 'Orders only') is very difficult because different content types require different date-extraction rules.

nodes.label: Epic accounts for approximately 70% of computerised electronic records

provenance.quote: Epic takes about 70% of them. So it consists for about 70% of electronic records.

nodes.statement: Epic is the source system for approximately 70% of computerised electronic records processed by MedBrief.

nodes.label: Current date filter applies at file level, not to individual documents within a file

provenance.quote: when you apply a filter currently, it filters according, it filters the file and not the actual document
if administrative documentation had multiple documents in it with different dates...I will still see all the documents that's in it, regardless whether it actually falls within that date falls or not

nodes.statement: The current records-page date filter applies to files (folders), not to individual documents within those files.

nodes.label: An applied date range on the records page is automatically propagated into Ask Alex as a filter

provenance.quote: if there's a date range that's applied here, it's automatically applied as a filter in Ask Alex
Of course, the user does have the option to remove it.

nodes.statement: Any date range applied on the records page is automatically applied as a filter in Ask Alex (MedBrief Insights), though users can remove it.

nodes.label: The date filter is a genuinely useful feature independent of MedBrief Insights

provenance.quote: This records the date filters independent of this feature as well, because it is, of course, useful, you know, for just whether we have MedBrief Insights or not.
This is actually a very useful feature for the users.

nodes.statement: The date-range filter on the records page is a valuable standalone feature regardless of whether MedBrief Insights is used.

nodes.label: Key events mode is hard to do accurately and is not in the MVP

provenance.quote: again, also very hard to do accurately.
what we are going to be doing, not for the MVP release

nodes.statement: Accurately generating key events from records is difficult, and key events as an AI-generated output is not included in the MVP release.

nodes.label: For the Gatsby Wix demo, a chronology was created on a matter and imported as key events

provenance.quote: that's what we did for the demo with Gatsby Wix. What we did was we created a chronology on a matter that didn't have a chronology and then imported the chronology as key events.

nodes.statement: For the Gatsby Wix demo, a chronology was manually created on a matter without one and imported as key events, as a workaround.

nodes.label: A full paid chronology can cost approximately £500

provenance.quote: we're talking about 500 pounds, more or less, plus, minus that it could cost

nodes.statement: A full MedBrief chronology service costs approximately £500 for a client.

nodes.label: Key events is intentionally less detailed than a paid chronology

provenance.quote: the key events, the idea there is that it's not as detailed as our existing chronologies for one
it's literally a text summary. It's got a heading and then it's got a bit of a blurb that describes it, but that's it.

nodes.statement: The key events feature is intentionally less detailed than a paid MedBrief chronology: it is a text summary with a heading and short description, without verbatim copy or specific references.

nodes.label: Competitive pressure from AI legal document providers motivates adding key events

provenance.quote: there's a lot of competition out there at the moment...there's a lot of like the legal documentation providers or providers, AI providers, that offer the same service.

nodes.statement: Competition from AI legal documentation and AI providers offering similar key-events services is a driver for MedBrief adding the key events feature.

nodes.label: ECA was previously done in Word; now done on the platform via wizard

provenance.quote: early case assessment, that was also something that was only written up in Word, created in Word. And then, not last year, the year before, we created a ECA wizard for the clinicians to do it on the platform.

nodes.statement: Early case assessments were previously written in Word; MedBrief subsequently built an ECA wizard to do them on the platform.

nodes.label: ECA wizard determines supportive / not supportive / inconclusive conclusion

provenance.quote: depending on what the clinician selects with each question, that at the end will determine whether an early case assessment is supportive, not supportive or inconclusive

nodes.statement: The ECA wizard aggregates clinician answers to breach-of-duty and causation questions and produces a conclusion of supportive, not supportive or inconclusive.

nodes.label: ECA workflow: allocated → ready for QA → QA'd → uploaded and released

provenance.quote: usually if the clinician is busy with it, it will be in progress, sorry, allocated. And then once they're done, it still needs to be QA'd. And then so it's ready for QA, then it gets QA'd. And then once QA is done, then it gets uploaded and released.

nodes.statement: The ECA status lifecycle is: allocated (clinician working) → ready for QA → QA'd → uploaded and released.

nodes.label: Storing ECA data in the database makes it reusable by the AI team, including for MedBrief Match

provenance.quote: it does get stored in the database, which makes it easier for the AI team to reference early case assessment data for whatever reason. And this is especially important for the next, one of the features we just made we've matched.

nodes.statement: Capturing ECA data on the platform stores it in the database, making it accessible to the AI team and usable by features such as MedBrief Match.

nodes.label: MedBrief migrated the radiology viewer from Orthanc to Medream last year

provenance.quote: We changed it last year. So we changed it from Orthanc to Medream.

nodes.statement: MedBrief replaced the Orthanc radiology viewer with Medream last year.

nodes.label: Radiology on dev environment was not working at time of demo; demo was shown on production

provenance.quote: I am now in production because apparently the radiology is not working. On dev.

nodes.statement: At the time of the walkthrough, the radiology viewer was non-functional on the dev environment, so Chantel demonstrated it on production.

nodes.label: The primary reason for migrating from Orthanc to Medream was broader radiology format support

provenance.quote: the biggest reason was because Orthanc, there was a lot of studies or radiology. Data formats or something. Yes. It didn't read.
But the biggest reason was support, format support.

nodes.statement: The primary driver for moving from Orthanc to Medream was that Orthanc could not read many radiology data formats; Medream supports a significantly wider range.

nodes.label: Medream supports PDF radiology reports in the viewer; Orthanc did not

provenance.quote: a lot of the time they send through reports, PDF reports. That's in, you know, that's readable in the viewer, whereas Orthanc doesn't do that. It doesn't support that.

nodes.statement: Unlike Orthanc, Medream can display PDF radiology reports directly inside the radiology viewer.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match is currently live only for select Lime users

provenance.quote: Medreamerge is currently only available for a select few users for one client, and that's Lime.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Match is in limited live deployment, enabled only for specific users within the Lime client.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match requires the matter to have been created via the matter creation wizard

provenance.quote: if it is a matter that was not created by the matter creation wizard, Medreamerge match will not be enabled there. Because the reason is for that is we do need it to be a newly created, not a newly created, but we do need to have it in the database.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Match only activates for matters created through the matter creation wizard (so the matter data is in the database).

nodes.label: MedBrief Match is not available for personal injury cases, only clinical negligence

provenance.quote: It will not work or be visible for personal injury cases.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Match is restricted to clinical negligence matters and does not appear for personal injury cases.

nodes.label: Expert profiles were AI-generated and verified from internet sources, not from opt-in

provenance.quote: We got AI and the clinicians to complete and verify the expert information on the internet. And we created profiles for those experts, for expert match to use

nodes.statement: MedBrief built expert profiles by having AI and clinicians research and verify expert information from the internet, rather than asking experts to opt in first.

nodes.label: No experts have complained about unsolicited contact via MedBrief Match so far

provenance.quote: So far, we haven't had any issues with experts complaining that they didn't want to receive instructions by med brief.

nodes.statement: As of the time of the walkthrough, no expert had complained about receiving unsolicited instructions via MedBrief Match.

nodes.label: Expert agencies charge 20–45% markup on the expert's fee; MedBrief Match plans to charge 8.5–14.5%

provenance.quote: the markup that they ask is really substantial. We're talking about anything between 20 to 45% markup. So, and that would be on top of the expert's fee.
we're thinking of only asking 8.5% of the expert's total fee. But we might be changing that a little bit higher, 14.5%.

nodes.statement: Traditional expert agencies charge a 20–45% markup on top of the expert's fee; MedBrief Match's planned markup is 8.5%, possibly rising to 14.5%, which has not yet been finalised.

nodes.label: The AI team recommends expert specialities and generates search queries based on matter information

provenance.quote: based on the matters information that we have, we actually recommend the experts that they need to consult.
What we also do generate with AI is we create these queries that the user can use.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Match uses matter data to AI-generate recommended expert specialities and natural-language search queries for the user.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match has a distinct colour palette to visually differentiate AI features

provenance.quote: you can see also the color palette for this is very different compared to the rest of the product. So we, yeah, so this was specific for expert match.

we're also going to be applying the same color to MedBreat Insights. Not as much though, not as drastic as what we're doing here, but at least for the button to stand out, as well as the logo that we're using, just bringing a little bit of the purple as well, because we want to tie in all the AI type peaches with this particular color palette.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Match uses a distinct purple-dominated colour palette, different from the rest of the product, to visually group all AI-type features. The same palette will be applied more subtly to MedBrief Insights.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match uses similar-case cross-referencing across all Match matters, but only shows client-specific case details

provenance.quote: we take the information on the matter and then we cross-reference that with the matters that are really on MedBreatMatch to see which other matters has experts worked on that's similar to this particular matters information.

while we are able to show the total count of similar cases that the expert has worked on across MedBreatMatch, we can only show them the details of line matters, of the client's matters for obvious reasons.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Match cross-references a matter against all Match matters to find similar cases; it shows the total similar-case count across the platform but restricts case detail to the current client's own matters.

nodes.label: Expert hourly rates and turnaround times in profiles are initially absent (AI-sourced profiles lack this); data is populated after expert contact

provenance.quote: Most of these don't have this information yet because we, like I said, we generated the expert's profile information from the internet. We don't have this information what the hourly rate is or the average turnaround time.
This one is an expert that's already been contacted via MedBreatMatch. So he obviously completed his hourly rate and his turnaround time.

nodes.statement: AI-sourced expert profiles initially lack hourly rate and turnaround time data; these fields are filled in once the expert has been contacted and responds via MedBrief Match.

nodes.label: Expert profile information from the internet is confirmed by the expert on first login

provenance.quote: once they've given their decision whether they can accept or decline, they get then prompted to confirm their profile information. Because obviously, we want to make sure that the profile information that we got on the expert is accurate.

nodes.statement: After an expert responds to a letter of approach, they are prompted to review and confirm the AI-generated profile information, at which point MedBrief treats it as accurate.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match AI-generates recommendation reasons for each expert

provenance.quote: we just give the reasons as to why this particular expert was recommended. And we do that for every single expert.

nodes.statement: For every expert returned in a MedBrief Match search, the platform AI-generates a set of recommendation reasons explaining why that expert is suited to the matter.

nodes.label: Letter of Approach uses a standard template; client customisation planned but will be added as client-specific templates, not UI self-service

provenance.quote: At the moment, the standard template will be used for all clients. But somewhere along the future, we will have functionality where the client can customize it.
So it will probably not be a UI thing where they can customize it themselves. They will need to let us know what they wanted to customize it as. And then we'll add a client-specific template.

nodes.statement: The letter of approach uses a single standard template for all clients currently; future customisation will be handled by MedBrief adding client-specific templates rather than offering a self-service UI editor.

nodes.label: Regex was tried for claimant-name replacement but abandoned; AI component needed instead

provenance.quote: The idea here was for us to use, we tried it with regular expression, but it's too tricky because the claimant name is not always as simple as the claimant name.
we may need the AM component to do that. So for the moment, it is unfortunately a very manual process

nodes.statement: MedBrief tried using regex to auto-replace the claimant name with initials in the letter of approach, but abandoned it because name formats vary; the team concluded an AI component may be needed, so for now it is a manual edit.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match requires sufficient matter information; disabled if below threshold

provenance.quote: only matters that have got sufficient information, we can use MedBeatMatch on.
at the moment, if it doesn't have sufficient information, we actually disable MedBriefMatch. MedBriefMatch. They'll see the page.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Match is disabled on matters lacking sufficient information; the page is visible but shows a message that the matter lacks sufficient information.

nodes.label: Sufficient-information criteria: background + key events above minimum character count, OR an ECA, chronology, or clinical summary

provenance.quote: the criteria for that is, is that a matter, you know, the, when we complete in the matter creation, whether it asks you for the background and as well as the key events. So that needs to be above a certain, a minimum character count.
Either that, or it needs an early case assessment or a chronology or a clinical summary or act.

nodes.statement: A matter qualifies for MedBrief Match if: (a) background + key events fields exceed a minimum character count, OR (b) the matter has an early case assessment, chronology, or clinical summary.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match was built quickly under a tight deadline; some features were deliberately left out for the initial Lime release

provenance.quote: This was created very, very quickly. We, it wasn't created at a very tight deadline. So there's a lot of the stuff that we, we left out just to, to get it out for this client.
And that was somewhere in March, I think, or beginning of April, we gave it to the client.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Match was built under a tight deadline; many features were deliberately omitted to get the initial release to Lime around March or April.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match is a new revenue stream and has been a long-standing client request

provenance.quote: it's literally another revenue stream, which is like amazing.
it's something that's been requested from clients in the past before is, you know, why can't we look for experts via make briefs?

nodes.statement: MedBrief Match represents a new revenue stream and has been a long-standing feature request from clients.

nodes.label: Letter of Approach PDF is encrypted and not sent as email attachment; expert must log in to view and respond

provenance.quote: the letter of approach PDF is generated, but it's not sent as an attachment in the email. The expert must, they must log on to, to view, view it.
The reason for that is because it needs to be encrypted, the PDF.

nodes.statement: The letter of approach PDF is generated and encrypted; it is not attached to the email sent to the expert. The expert must log in to the platform to view and respond to it.

nodes.label: Whole letter-of-approach process (search, send, expert response) is end-to-end within MedBrief

provenance.quote: So does the whole process end to end, like the searching for them, sending the letter of approach, does this all happen within MedBrief?
Correct.

nodes.statement: The entire expert-matching workflow from search through letter of approach to expert acceptance or decline is conducted within the MedBrief platform, facilitated by system-generated emails.

nodes.label: Claimant name / initials choice propagates to email subject, email body and expert's matter view

provenance.quote: It applies to the subject line of the email or the content of the email that gets sent to the, to the expert where he will not see the patient or the claimant's full name.
also when they log in, because he doesn't have access to this matter yet, even though you can see this matter widget, he only still sees the, the initials.

nodes.statement: Selecting initials vs full name in the letter of approach applies throughout: the email subject, email body, and what the expert sees when they log in before being invited to the matter.

nodes.label: Expert must agree to MedBrief terms and conditions to accept, decline, or request more information

provenance.quote: whether they decline, accept, or they request any more information for them to, to be able to continue, they have to, to have to agree to the terms and conditions, which is a bit risky because if I'm an expert and I'm declining, I don't necessarily want to agree to terms and conditions
We are trying this and just see, um, hopefully there won't be too much of a drop off.

nodes.statement: Regardless of their decision, experts must agree to the MedBrief expert user terms and conditions before they can accept, decline, or request more information.

nodes.label: Risk: requiring T&Cs agreement even for declining experts may cause drop-off

provenance.quote: which is a bit risky because if I'm an expert and I'm declining, I don't necessarily want to agree to terms and conditions because I'm not, anyway. But we are trying this and just see, um, hopefully there won't be too much of a drop off.

nodes.statement: Requiring experts to agree to terms and conditions even when declining is acknowledged as a risk that could cause expert drop-off.

nodes.label: Required-information requests loop back to the decision page on every return until accept or decline is given

provenance.quote: every, every single time they come back and they need to, to give an answer, they will be directed to this page every single time until they give us an accept or decline.

nodes.statement: If an expert requests more information before deciding, they are redirected to the decision page every time they log in until they have given a definitive accept or decline.

nodes.label: Experts frequently reply to the letter of approach email directly rather than logging in, causing a manual copy-paste process

provenance.quote: experts, they, when they receive a letter of approach by make brief, instead of them logging on to respond to them, to their email, they reply to the email. So at the moment, that's a manual process.
whoever gets that email needs to, or usually MNE, she needs to update the expert's response accordingly and then copy and paste whatever the email contains into the actual conversations

nodes.statement: It is a known and frequent problem that experts reply to the notification email directly rather than logging into MedBrief; the result is a manual process where staff must copy the email content into the platform conversation.

nodes.label: Letter of instruction is not yet created inside the platform; clients generate it themselves and attach it manually

provenance.quote: No, it's different. Um, but it is something that we are going to do down the line.
at the moment, the clients need to generate that themselves.

nodes.statement: The letter of instruction is currently generated outside the platform by the client and manually attached as a message; in-platform creation is planned for the future.

nodes.label: Expert invitation email address is hidden from solicitors; solicitors cannot see expert email even on copy emails

provenance.quote: whenever a solicitor contacts an expert via made brief match, they are not aware of what that particular expert's email address is in any way. We don't show it to them in any way.
Even the email that they get copied on that goes to the expert, they don't see us.

nodes.statement: Solicitors using MedBrief Match never see the expert's email address; even CC copies of the notification email redact it.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match is live but usage is very low: only three users and seven matters so far

provenance.quote: So the progress on this at the moment is very, very slow. Because I think it's only about, it's only been three users that's actually used it.
it's only been used on one, two, three, four, five, six, seven matters.

nodes.statement: As of the walkthrough date, MedBrief Match has been used by approximately three users across seven matters.

nodes.label: Two expert reports have been completed via MedBrief Match; at least one billing cycle has occurred with Lime

provenance.quote: We've had two reports completed so far by experts.
Invoiced. Yeah. Or it's, at least it's worth, it's either been done or it's worth the accounts department to sort that out.

nodes.statement: Two expert reports have been completed via MedBrief Match. Invoicing to Lime has either been done or is being handled by the accounts department.

nodes.label: Invite suggestions on the users page only show experts who had prior dealings with that specific client

provenance.quote: a user only come up if it's found, if that user, or in this case, the expert had previous dealings with this particular client in the past.

nodes.statement: When inviting a user from the users page, auto-suggestions only display people who have previously had dealings with that specific client; super-admins always see all users.

nodes.label: Disclosures are between opposing law firms (claimant/defendant), not MedBrief-managed
nodes.statement: Disclosures are where one firm discloses all the records it holds to the opposing firm, whether claimant to defendant or defendant to claimant. This is entirely client-managed and cuts out MedBrief admin.

provenance.quote: So it's where the one firm discloses all the records that they have to the opposing firm. So whether it's claimant to defendant or defendant to claimant, that's what this is.
this is not a MedBrief admin thing. This is a client thing. So clients can go and do this themselves.

nodes.label: Disclosure fee is approximately £35-£45, payable by either the disclosing or recipient firm
nodes.statement: MedBrief charges a fee of approximately £35 or £45 for a disclosure. Depending on configuration, this fee can fall on the firm creating the disclosure or on the recipient firm.

provenance.quote: we do ask a fee, I think it's 35 pounds or 45 pounds for disclosure
sometimes it's the firm that whose matter this is who's doing the disclosure, sometimes they opt to pay the fee or sometimes it's the recipient fee that needs to pay the fee

nodes.label: Disclosure recipients are notified by email and access the limited record set via MedBrief login
nodes.statement: When a disclosure is confirmed, recipients receive an email. Existing MedBrief users get a direct login link; new users are prompted to activate their profile by choosing a password. They then see only the document and radiology viewers limited to the disclosed records.

provenance.quote: an email will go out to all the parties that have been invited, as well as the person who created this invitation or disclosure
If they're not already a user on MedBrief based on that email address, then they get asked to activate their profile where they just choose a password.
they get to see the document viewer and the radiology viewer, et cetera, but all limited to just the stuff that's been disclosed

nodes.label: Disclosure status lifecycle is: pending → processing → active
nodes.statement: After a disclosure is confirmed it starts with a status of 'pending', then moves to 'processing', and finally becomes 'active'. The transition to active takes a little while.

provenance.quote: it's going to have the status of pending, then processing. And then once it's active, it will update to active. But that takes a little while.

nodes.label: Disclosures expire after 30 days and can be reactivated for a further 30 days
nodes.statement: A disclosure has a 30-day validity period from creation. Once it expires it can be reactivated for another 30-day period, and records/users can be updated while active.

provenance.quote: when a disclosures created, there's a 30-day period before the disclosure actually expires
Once they have expired, they can be reactivated for another 30 days or whatever.

nodes.label: Communications tab links emails to a matter via matter reference number or records-request keyword in the subject line
nodes.statement: Any email to or from medical institutions or clients is linked to the relevant matter page if its subject line contains either the matter reference number or the words 'records request'. The tab is only visible to MedBrief admins.

provenance.quote: as long as it has either the matter reference number or a records request in the subject line, it will link it to this page
The communications tab is only visible to MedBrief admins.

nodes.label: Saved reports drive the ops team's daily workflow across records requests, batches, clinical reports, and new matters
nodes.statement: The reports section (admin-only) contains saved reports used daily by the ops team to identify follow-up work. They cover records requests, batches, clinical reports, additional requests, sorting sessions and matter requests.

provenance.quote: what determines the workflow of matters that get worked on is determined by the various reports
They have got saved reports that they use on a daily basis. So they'll have like a daily chase.
For batches, same thing. For clinical reports, if they need to keep track on what's outstanding, what's overdue, that type of thing, they use the clinical reports.

nodes.label: Matter requests report only includes matters created via the matter creation wizard, not legacy-created matters
nodes.statement: Any new matter created by the matter creation wizard is added to the matter requests report for client services review. Matters created the legacy way are not included.

provenance.quote: Any new matter that's created by the matter creation wizard, I should correct. If it's created the legacy way, it will not be added here.

nodes.label: Firms section is for API-based matter import; only Kennedys currently uses it
nodes.statement: The Firms section in Manage is not the same as Clients. It is specifically for API-based matter import. Currently only Kennedys uses an API to import matters from their own A-System.

provenance.quote: It's actually for an API. So we've only got one firm, which is Kennedy, that uses an API to import the matters that they created on the A-System.

nodes.label: Clinical sections list has 146 approved entries; unapproved ones accumulate from DocSorter operators adding new values
nodes.statement: There are 146 approved clinical sections available in the DocSorter dropdown. Operators can add new clinical sections on the fly, which is why the unfiltered list had ~15,000 entries, but only approved ones appear in the dropdown.

provenance.quote: there's 146. That's much better. So you get your clinical sections that's approved, that's added to the dropdown whenever they select a clinical section.
If it's not a clinical section that's in the dropdown, they can obviously add it. And that's why you've seen 15,000 in the original list.

nodes.label: Expert agency notification on MedBrief was used previously but is no longer needed
nodes.statement: MedBrief used to notify expert agencies when an expert linked to their agency was instructed or invited on MedBrief. That notification behaviour has since been removed as it was deemed unnecessary.

provenance.quote: we actually used to... notify that particular agency that is linked to that expert that this expert was instructed on MedBrief or invited on MedBrief. But we've since, well, MedBrief matches actually need that.

nodes.label: Client-specific billing rates can be configured; current default is 8.5% markup for all clients
nodes.statement: Billing items can be set at a universal rate or per client. The current default is an 8.5% markup applied to all clients, but some clients with preferential sorting rates have client-specific billing items.

provenance.quote: They can also create client-specific billing items, which they do.
it's just they all use the same 8.5% markup

nodes.label: Invoices are generated in MedBrief and then synced to Xero, which sends the invoice to the client
nodes.statement: The MedBrief accounts team creates invoices from completed service requests in the manage invoices section, then links them to Xero. Xero sends the actual invoice to the client.

provenance.quote: once they're done creating the invoice, then they link it to zero. And then whatever they created here will then be copied over to zero. And then zero will send out the invoice to wherever it needs to go.

nodes.label: Failed logins trigger a 15-minute lockout; admins can clear it manually
nodes.statement: After a certain number of incorrect login attempts a user is blocked for 15 minutes. The manage failed-logins page lists these cases and admins can clear the lockout by deleting the entry.

provenance.quote: if a user logs in incorrectly a certain amount of times, we blacklist. It would be blocked in from logging in for 15 minutes.
You can clear it by just deleting it.

nodes.label: Help articles show content specific to user type (client vs expert/disclosure expert)
nodes.statement: The help articles section shows different content depending on the user's type: clients see client-specific content, experts and disclosure (third-party) experts see their relevant content, and some articles apply to both.

provenance.quote: There's different content displays for different user types. So if it's a client, they'll see specific content. If it's an expert or disclosure expert, which is third party, they'll see specific content.

nodes.label: Users cannot change their own email address; it requires a dev team database request
nodes.statement: Users cannot update their own email address in the profile page. Email address changes must be made directly in the database by the development team following a user request.

provenance.quote: users can't change the email addresses themselves. If they want to change, we can do it for them via the database. So it needs to be a request from the dev team.

nodes.label: Folder downloads are processed in the background and collected from the profile page downloads section
nodes.statement: Single document downloads go directly to the user's device. Folder downloads (and large radiology downloads) are processed in the background; the resulting file is available from the downloads section of the user's profile page.

provenance.quote: if they download a document via the records page, for example, that gets downloaded to the user's device straight away.
If they download a folder, however, it gets repaired in the background and the user will be able to get it here.

nodes.label: MSR product notes user-role matrix is more complete than the in-product help version (includes admins and super-admins)
nodes.statement: The internal MSR product notes contain a fuller user-role matrix that includes MedBrief admin and super-admin roles, compared to the help area matrix which only covers client and matter roles.

provenance.quote: this is a much broader user role matrix than what is in the help area. So this does include maybe admins and super admins.

nodes.label: Private Folders root folder to be renamed (to 'Workspace' or similar) as part of parked project

provenance.quote: We're actually going to change that name, but anyway.
One of which is changing private folders, the term to, to work. What was it? Workspace. Workspace or something like that.

nodes.statement: The 'Private Folders' root folder will be renamed to 'Workspace' (or similar) as part of the parked improvements project.

nodes.label: Folders in Case Documentation to be grouped by user type (internal, client, expert)

provenance.quote: we're going to be grouping the users to internal, you know, by internal users and client users and expert users. So that it's easier to, to identify the different folders.
that still needs all of that functionality still needs to be implemented. It's taken, it's been parked.

nodes.statement: Users' folders within Case Documentation will be grouped by type (internal, client, expert) rather than listed alphabetically, to make identification easier.
nodes.rationale: Current alphabetical listing makes it hard to distinguish folder types, especially for admins and clients.

nodes.label: MedBrief Insights panel will not go live until the AI team confirms sufficient model accuracy

provenance.quote: they are reviewing the model of accuracy to see how accurate it is because if it's not very accurate, it's not something we will be able to take live yet until it is.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Insights / Ask Alex will not be released to production until the AI team has reviewed and confirmed the model's accuracy is sufficient.
nodes.rationale: An inaccurate AI assistant would be harmful to users in a medico-legal context.

nodes.label: Use bookmarks (or VLM heading fallback) instead of clinical section identification for CER index

provenance.quote: we first thought about identifying the different clinical sections in the document. But that is very difficult...So instead, what we're doing for now, at least, is we're extracting the bookmarks

nodes.statement: The CER index approach pivoted from attempting to identify clinical sections to extracting bookmarks (or headings via VLM as fallback) because clinical section identification by content is too difficult.
nodes.rationale: Clinical section identification is too difficult because content varies and multiple sections can appear on one page; bookmarks are familiar to users and already used.
alternatives_considered: Identifying clinical sections by content type

nodes.label: Launch CER index on Epic only first, extend to other CER types later

provenance.quote: we're going to go live with the solution, the CER index, only on Epic. Because for all the computerized electronic records, Epic takes about 70% of them.
So for that reason, we're only going to go with Epic. And then later, we will attempt the other CER types.

nodes.statement: The CER index will launch exclusively on Epic, with other CER types attempted later.
nodes.rationale: Epic covers approximately 70% of computerised electronic records, making it the highest-value first target.

nodes.label: Key events not included in MVP; chronology wizard to be built first

provenance.quote: what we are going to be doing, not for the MVP release, we're going to create a chronology wizard
And then at some point, once we actually can do key events without the help of chronology, then the AI team will add key events as another mode or future for metric insights.

nodes.statement: Key events as an AI-generated output is excluded from the MVP release. A chronology wizard will be built first; key events will be added as a future MedBrief Insights mode once it can be done without relying on an existing chronology.
nodes.rationale: Key events is hard to do accurately without a chronology; the chronology wizard must come first to provide structured input.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match markup fee not yet finalised; considering 8.5% or 14.5% of expert's total fee

provenance.quote: we're thinking of only asking 8.5% of the expert's total fee. But we might be changing that a little bit higher, 14.5%. So it's like I said, it's not been confirmed yet.

nodes.statement: The MedBrief Match service markup has not been finalised; the options under consideration are 8.5% or 14.5% of the expert's total fee.

nodes.label: Apply MedBrief Match purple colour palette to MedBrief Insights (subtly) to visually unify AI features

provenance.quote: we're also going to be applying the same color to MedBreat Insights. Not as much though, not as drastic as what we're doing here, but at least for the button to stand out, as well as the logo that we're using, just bringing a little bit of the purple as well, because we want to tie in all the AI type peaches with this particular color palette.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Insights will receive a subtle version of the MedBrief Match purple colour palette (primarily on the button and logo) to visually tie all AI-type features together.
nodes.rationale: Establish a consistent visual identity across AI features.

nodes.label: Display total similar-case count across all Match matters, but restrict case details to the client's own matters

provenance.quote: we are going to be changing it that we display the total number of cases regardless of the clients. But then when you view the profile, you'll see in a moment when the client views the profile, we actually show them the details of that similar case. So while we are able to show the total count of similar cases that the expert has worked on across MedBreatMatch, we can only show them the details of line matters, of the client's matters for obvious reasons.

nodes.statement: MedBrief Match will display the total number of similar cases an expert has worked on across the whole platform, but restrict the detailed case view to the current client's own matters.
nodes.rationale: Privacy: case details of other clients' matters cannot be exposed.

nodes.label: Letter of Approach PDF delivered encrypted via platform login, not as email attachment

provenance.quote: the letter of approach PDF is generated, but it's not sent as an attachment in the email. The expert must, they must log on to, to view, view it. The reason for that is because it needs to be encrypted, the PDF.

nodes.statement: The letter of approach PDF is not sent as an email attachment; it is encrypted and accessible only after the expert logs in to the platform.
nodes.rationale: Encryption requirement and forcing expert login to enable the response workflow.

nodes.label: T&Cs required from expert for any response (accept, decline, or request more info) despite drop-off risk

provenance.quote: whether they decline, accept, or they request any more information for them to, to be able to continue, they have to, to have to agree to the terms and conditions
We are trying this and just see, um, hopefully there won't be too much of a drop off.

nodes.statement: The decision was made to require experts to accept MedBrief terms and conditions regardless of their response type (accept, decline or request info), even though this carries a drop-off risk for declining experts.

nodes.label: Expert 'instructed' status inferred from invitation event rather than an explicit instructed flag

provenance.quote: we are using the fact that the moment an expert is invited, we're going to be using that as the instructed status. Because the only time an expert is invited is when they have been instructed.

nodes.statement: In the absence of an explicit instructed status, the system infers instructed status from the moment the expert is invited to the matter.
nodes.rationale: An expert is only ever invited after being instructed, making the invite event a reliable proxy.

nodes.label: Expert agency notification discontinued — MedBrief no longer notifies agencies when linked experts are instructed
nodes.statement: MedBrief has removed the behaviour of notifying expert agencies when an expert linked to them is instructed or invited on MedBrief, having determined it is no longer needed.
nodes.rationale: MedBrief concluded the agency notification served no necessary purpose.

provenance.quote: we've since, well, MedBrief matches actually need that. So at the moment, there's no link or benefit really to have that label.

provenance.quote: ideally, at some point, hopefully we can change that, that it only gets uploaded once by the clients and it gets distributed where it needs to go.
we have a very, very long wish list of things we'd like to do. And yeah, it's just, they never get to see the light of day pretty much.

nodes.source_quote: ideally, at some point, hopefully we can change that, that it only gets uploaded once by the clients and it gets distributed where it needs to go.

He might have mentioned it to you, and I think he'll probably rope it in to get your input on a solution for this as well.

regardless of where that page, if that page number changes or the position of the page changes in the sorted records, we remember which page it belongs to.

nodes.description: Dion is building a solution to save annotation and redaction coordinates at a granular level so they survive record updates where page numbers or positions change.
nodes.source_quote: the project that Dion is busy with is the granular storing so that if annotations are made or redaction, you know, sections have been redacted, for us to be able to save the coordinates of whatever was implemented.

nodes.label: Does the CER index extract headings or bookmarks?

provenance.quote: The headings, not the bookmarks.

nodes.text: Rowan clarifying: the CER index extracts headings, not bookmarks?
nodes.answer_summary: Both: bookmarks are extracted if present; VLM heading extraction is the fallback.

nodes.label: Does the keyword search look through the whole document or only heading content?

provenance.quote: So basically find the headings that have content beneath that heading, or within that headings section that match that text, and then return the heading.

nodes.text: Does the keyword search look through the full document or only the content associated with extracted headings?
nodes.answer_summary: Searches only the content of headings listed in the index, not the entire document.

nodes.label: Does the patient section header carry a date for use in date filtering?

provenance.quote: Under the patient header, and like, as an example, that was higher up there, like, that didn't have a date in it. So does that actually have a date, like, applied to it?

nodes.text: Does the patient section header have a date applied to it so it would be returned by date filtering?
nodes.answer_summary: No. The patient section cannot be dated because it is a summary of information throughout the document; demographics, contacts, problem lists and allergies are not dateable items.

nodes.label: What sections appear in a CER beyond the patient and repeatable event sections?

provenance.quote: So the CER output there, you had a patient section, and then you have like a repeatable event section. And then are there other types of sections as well?

nodes.text: Are there CER section types beyond the patient section and the repeatable event section?
nodes.answer_summary: Yes: ancillary orders, plastic surgery appointments, documentation (no date), and orders only (no date in heading but dates in content).

nodes.label: For sections with dates embedded in content rather than headings, should those dates be extracted to date the section?

provenance.quote: So in which case we'd want to find it and then date that section.

nodes.text: Where a section has no date in its heading but has dates in content (e.g. Orders only), should MedBrief extract those content dates to date the section?
nodes.answer_summary: Yes, ideally, but it is very difficult because content-embedded dates vary in type and meaning; the AI team wants a generalised solution.

nodes.label: Does the CER structure (heading format) apply across all CER provider systems or only Epic?

provenance.quote: And that structure that was in your sheet, is that representative of the CER records that come from all those different providers? Or is this just Epic?

nodes.text: Is the CER heading/section structure seen in the reference sheet representative of all CER provider systems, or specific to Epic?
nodes.answer_summary: Only Epic at launch; other CER types will be attempted later and may need different models.

provenance.quote: what purpose does the key events, like, let's say we could generate key events either with chronologies on a case or no chronologies. Like, who wants to see those? Why do they want to see them? And what use are the key events to somebody?

nodes.text: What is the purpose of the key events feature and who are the intended users?
nodes.answer_summary: Key events provides a lighter, lower-cost overview of the timeline of events in a matter, positioned between having nothing and paying for a full chronology, also responding to competitive pressure from AI legal document providers.

nodes.label: Who creates the Figma designs for MedBrief?

provenance.quote: Who does these Figma? Designs, by the way.

nodes.text: Who is responsible for the Figma design mockups shown?
nodes.answer_summary: Anna, a freelancer engaged half-day three times a week.

nodes.label: Does the ECA progress indicator track which wizard questions have been answered?

provenance.quote: So that early case assessment on the dashboard there had like a progress thing. So does that progress through which of those questions on that page you've answered?

nodes.text: Does the progress indicator shown on the ECA dashboard record represent progress through the individual wizard questions?
nodes.answer_summary: No. The status indicates the workflow stage (allocated, ready for QA, QA'd, uploaded and released), not which questions have been answered.

nodes.label: Why did MedBrief move from Orthanc to Medream?

provenance.quote: Do you know why we moved from Orthanc onto, what was it called? Medream.

nodes.text: Why did MedBrief migrate the radiology viewer from Orthanc to Medream?
nodes.answer_summary: Primarily broader radiology format support — Orthanc could not read many data formats. Medream also supports PDF radiology reports inside the viewer.

nodes.label: How do clients find experts without MedBrief Match?

provenance.quote: How do our clients find experts otherwise?

nodes.text: How do MedBrief clients currently find medical experts without using MedBrief Match?
nodes.answer_summary: Clients either use their own list of previously-used experts or go through an expert agency, which can charge 20–45% markup on the expert's fee.

nodes.label: Does MedBrief Match show similar-case results in the UI?

provenance.quote: And you also show them here or do you show them?

nodes.text: Does MedBrief Match surface similar-case information to the user in the results view?
nodes.answer_summary: Yes. The total similar-case count is shown on the expert card across all Match matters; case details are shown only for the client's own matters.

nodes.label: Could matters without sufficient info still allow browsing experts by speciality?

provenance.quote: I imagine it would still be technically possible for, even if you had matters that didn't have any of those things and therefore you weren't able to generate backgrounds and recommended experts, it, it would still be useful for, for clients to just be able to like browse experts based on their own selection of speciality and just find people and pay.

nodes.text: Could matters without sufficient information for MedBrief Match still allow clients to browse and shortlist experts manually by selecting their own speciality?
nodes.answer_summary: Not at the moment. If a matter lacks sufficient information, MedBrief Match is disabled. The page displays with an 'insufficient information' message. Chantel acknowledged it should theoretically be possible to browse independently, but this feature does not exist yet.

nodes.label: Is the entire search-to-response process end-to-end within MedBrief?

provenance.quote: So does the whole process end to end, like the searching for them, sending the letter of approach, does this all happen within MedBrief? And then the person gets their email, the expert gets their email, they log into MedBrief, they can see the letter, and then they can, uh, they can accept or decline within, within MedBrief.

nodes.text: Is the entire process from searching for an expert, sending the letter of approach, and the expert accepting or declining all handled within MedBrief, facilitated by system-generated emails?
nodes.answer_summary: Correct. Chantel confirmed the entire workflow is on-platform with system-generated emails.

nodes.label: Is the letter of instruction also created inside the platform like the letter of approach?

provenance.quote: Sorry, Chantelle, the letter of instruction, is that also created inside the platform here in a similar way to creating the letter of approach or is that different?

nodes.text: Is the letter of instruction created inside the MedBrief platform in a similar manner to the letter of approach?
nodes.answer_summary: No. The letter of instruction is different and is not yet created inside the platform. It is planned for the future. Clients currently generate it themselves and attach it manually.

nodes.label: How does a disclosure get to the recipient — is it handled by MedBrief staff or is it automatic?
nodes.text: So does that mean, so does that come in, is there somebody then at MedBrief who handles these disclosures? Like they'll get notified and then they like send, send it to them? Or does it automatic? How does it, how does the disclosure get to the recipient?
nodes.answer_summary: Recipients receive an email. Existing MedBrief users get a direct login link; new users are asked to activate their profile. MedBrief staff are not needed unless there is an issue — it is automated.

provenance.quote: is there somebody then at MedBrief who handles these disclosures? Like they'll get notified and then they like send it to them? Or does it automatic?

nodes.label: Records page permission model: super-admin, expert and client folder views
nodes.summary: Recap of the records page, how personal folders are created under both case documentation and private folders, and what super-admins, experts and clients each see. Covers the parked project to rename private folders to workspace and group users by internal, client and expert.

nodes.label: Unsorted records folder, client uploads and folder actions
nodes.summary: The unsorted records folder where client-uploaded records land, the duplication this causes for the ops team, and folder actions such as rename, delete, download and notify. Includes the suppressed-invitation case where notification is replaced by sending the invitation.

nodes.label: Records viewer (Apryse), CER bookmarks and the annotation/redaction problem
nodes.summary: The Apryse (formerly PDFTron) records viewer: bookmark and heading navigation of long computerised electronic records, why MedBrief does not classify or date CER sections, and the annotations and redaction feature. Explains how updates lose annotations and Dion's granular-storing project to preserve coordinates across page changes.

nodes.label: MedBrief Insights AI panel and Ask Alex mode
nodes.summary: Introduction to the in-development MedBrief Insights AI panel and its primary Ask Alex mode for interrogating MedBrief-sorted records, with file and folder filters, example questions, references and a feedback mechanism. Notes the chat interface was built in-house and the AI team is still reviewing model accuracy.

nodes.label: CER index mode: heading extraction, date and keyword filtering
nodes.summary: The reworked CER index that extracts Epic bookmarks or, failing that, headings via a visual language model, presented as an interactive index with date-range and keyword filtering over heading content. Covers patient versus event sections, date-extraction rules per event type, and the decision to launch on Epic first as it is roughly 70% of CER records.

nodes.label: Records date-range filter on files and documents
nodes.summary: How the records-page date range works: each document and folder carries a date range from DocSorter, but the filter currently applies at file level rather than to documents within a file. Dion is to extend it to filter documents inside files, and any applied date range auto-carries into Ask Alex.

nodes.label: Key events mode and the planned chronology wizard
nodes.summary: The key events mode that lists a timeline of key events from the records, and why it is hard to do accurately. Covers the plan to build a chronology wizard so clinicians generate chronologies in MedBrief as a hyperlinked digital form rather than in Word, which would later feed key events.

nodes.label: Key events positioning versus paid chronologies, and Figma mockups
nodes.summary: Why key events exists as a lighter overview sitting between nothing and a paid chronology, framed against competition from AI legal-document providers and the cost of a full chronology. Reviews Figma designs by freelancer Anna for the key events view and the updated CER index.

nodes.label: Early Case Assessment wizard
nodes.summary: The ECA wizard that moved early case assessments from Word onto the platform, with structured questions on breach of duty and causation whose answers determine a supportive, not supportive or inconclusive conclusion. Notes that storing ECA data in the database makes it reusable by the AI team, for example for MedBrief Match.

nodes.label: Radiology viewer migration from Orthanc to Medream
nodes.summary: The radiology viewer, demonstrated in production, and the move from Orthanc to Medream. The main driver was broader format support, plus the ability to read PDF radiology reports inside the viewer, alongside study loading and measurement features.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match: expert-matching concept and enablement
nodes.summary: Introduction to MedBrief Match (Medream Merge) for finding experts, currently live for select Lime users only, with enablement conditions such as matter-wizard creation and clinical-negligence cases. Covers AI-generated expert profiles sourced from the internet, the letter-of-approach approach, the cost advantage over expert agencies, and AI-recommended specialities and queries.

nodes.label: MedBrief Match results, similar-case matching and expert profiles
nodes.summary: The expert search results grouped by speciality, including similar-case matching that cross-references the matter against other MedBrief Match matters. Walks through profile cards, hourly rate and turnaround data, filters, and AI-generated recommendation reasons for each expert.

nodes.label: Generating and sending the letter of approach
nodes.summary: Creating a letter of approach: standard template, the choice to show the claimant's full name or initials, AI-generated background and records of tape, attachments requested by Lime, and the criteria for sufficient matter information. The PDF is encrypted and viewed only after the expert logs in rather than sent as an attachment.

nodes.label: Expert experience: messages page, user simulation and responding to a letter of approach
nodes.summary: The new messages navigation item and how a super-admin can simulate an expert user to see what they receive. Walks through the expert responding to a letter of approach with the eight client questions, the accept, decline or request-more-information flow, mandatory terms and conditions, and profile confirmation.

nodes.label: Letter of instruction, email-reply gap and billing automation plans
nodes.summary: What happens after acceptance: the letter of instruction (created outside the platform for now), the manual handling of experts who reply by email, and the plan to invite the expert directly from the match page so the instructed status is captured. Also covers planned record-upload prompts to track report and invoice submission and automated billing service requests.

nodes.label: Inviting users and experts, roles and suppressed invitations
nodes.summary: Inviting users from the users page, how suggestions only appear for people with prior dealings with the client, and the available roles including expert, view-only expert, scanner and matter project manager. Demonstrates suppressing an invitation to pre-create an expert folder and the planned MedBrief Match marker on contacted experts.

nodes.label: Disclosures: inter-party record sharing
nodes.summary: Disclosures, where one firm discloses records and radiology to an opposing firm, created through a client-managed wizard with a fee whose liability can fall on either firm. Covers recipient access by email or activation, the 30-day expiry with reactivation, and updating the disclosed record set.

nodes.label: Communications tab and workflow-driving reports
nodes.summary: The admin-only communications tab that links emails to a matter via reference number or records request, and the reports section that drives the ops team's workflow. Saved reports such as records-request daily chases, batches, clinical reports and new matter requests determine and track outstanding work.

nodes.label: Manage section: clients, DocSorter config, service options and invoicing
nodes.summary: The admin manage section covering clients, firms (API import via Kennedys), matters, users and incomplete matters, plus DocSorter configuration such as clinical sections, sorting instructions and file-note phrases. Also covers expert agencies, records request centres, service options and timelines, billing items including the MedBrief Match markup, and generating invoices that sync to Xero.

nodes.label: Admin tools, profile page and product-notes handover
nodes.summary: The remaining admin area: system messages, the radiology status panel, restoring deleted documents, blacklist and failed-login management, and help articles with the user-role matrix. Closes with the profile page (email changes, Microsoft single sign-on, MFA, login logs and downloads) and a handover of internal product-notes lookup pages.

nodes.label: Letter of Approach - AI-drafted editor

provenance.quote: Letter of Approach. Dr Alastair Bint - Freelance GP and General Practice Expert Witness. Matter: Mr Darren Joseph -v- United Lincolnshire Hospitals NHS Trust. Matter Number: 2544944.1. Matter Manager: Layla Said. Dear Dr Bint, I act on behalf of [Claimant initials] in relation to a potential clinical negligence claim against United Lincolnshire Hospitals NHS Trust. I am writing to enquire whether you would be willing and available to accept instructions in this matter to prepare a report addressing issues of breach of duty and causation. Background: The background facts are as follows... The key issues requiring expert analysis include: 1. The clinical circumstances and management decisions relevant to the alleged breach; 2. The potential causal relationship between the alleged breach and any reported injury or outcome. Records obtained: We have obtained the following medical records to date - [Chapter] pages from the Claimant's GP surgery, [Chapter] pages from [Hospital Trust Name], [Chapter] pages from other relevant providers. [Update required]. Progress of litigation: We are presently at an early stage of the litigation. Helper prompt: 'How did we do with the case summary?'. Buttons: Save & preview, Save draft, Review & send.

nodes.label: Matter overview - Brienne Marston (matter details & background)

Matter Background: 2011 cervical screening / colposcopy narrative (HPV, repeat colposcopy 2020, hysterectomy 2024, missed opportunity for diagnosis). Right panel: Records requests (0/0); Batches (3/3); Clinical reports (0/0); Additional requests (0/0); Sorting sessions (0/0). Matter notes: All notes (25). Manage options / Download.

nodes.description: A matter dashboard for 'Brienne Marston -v- University Hospitals Coventry & Warwickshire NHS Trust' showing matter details, a clinical 'Matter Background' narrative, and collapsible counters for Records requests, Batches (3/3), Clinical reports, Additional requests and Sorting sessions. Shows the central matter hub tying together records workflow and case context.

provenance.quote: The MedBrief Insights chat interface was built in-house from scratch, not sourced from a third-party library
The entire MedBrief Insights panel, including Ask Alex, is currently in development and not yet live in production
The CER index extracts bookmarks where they exist; if not, it uses a visual language model to extract headings from the document visually
The keyword search in the CER index searches only the content of extracted headings, not the full body text of the documents
The patient section headings cannot be filtered by date because they contain no date; they are demographic and static
Day-visit events return a single date; admission events return a date range
Extracting dates from sections with content-embedded dates (such as Orders only) is very difficult
Epic is the source system for approximately 70% of computerised electronic records processed by MedBrief
The key events feature is intentionally less detailed than a paid MedBrief chronology: it provides a lightweight AI-generated summary of key events as a quick overview
The primary driver for moving from Orthanc to Medream was that Orthanc could not read many radiology file formats
Traditional expert agencies charge a 20-45% markup on top of the expert's fee; MedBrief Match offers a lower-cost alternative
MedBrief Match cross-references a matter against all Match matters to find similar cases; the number of similar cases is surfaced on the expert card
MedBrief Match is disabled on matters lacking sufficient information; the page is visible but the search is blocked with an explanation
The entire expert-matching workflow from search through letter of approach to expert acceptance is handled within the MedBrief platform
The letter of instruction is currently generated outside the platform by the client and manually sent
It is a known and frequent problem that experts reply to the notification email directly rather than using the platform
Automatically identifying clinical sections by content is very difficult because content varies enormously across CER types
Epic is the source system for approximately 70% of computerised electronic records processed by MedBrief
Accurately generating key events from records is difficult, and key events as an AI-generated output is still being refined
Competition from AI legal documentation and AI providers offering similar key-events services is a concern
MedBrief Match was built under a tight deadline; many features were deliberately omitted to ship on time
MedBrief Match represents a new revenue stream and has been a long-standing feature request
Requiring experts to agree to terms and conditions even when declining is acknowledged as slightly suboptimal but was a deliberate decision
If bookmarks do not exist the CER index uses a visual language model to extract headings from the document visually
Epic-type CER documents are bookmarked according to their heading hierarchy and the viewer navigates these bookmarks
MedBrief's DocSorter process classifies CER documents as a whole type but does not date them or classify their internal sections
Create the chronology wizard allowing clinicians to generate chronologies in MedBrief as a digital hyperlinked form

Extend the records-page date-range filter so that it applies to individual documents within a file; the AI team aims to develop a generalised date-extraction model rather than writing specific per-section rules
Automate the creation of a billing service request in the clinical report section when a letter of instruction is generated on the platform
Build in-platform generation of the letter of instruction; the system infers instructed status from the letter of instruction being generated where no explicit status exists
Dion is building a solution to save annotation and redaction coordinates at a granular level; the Insights panel will not be released until the AI team has reviewed accuracy
One of Dion's two current projects, possibly the intra-file document date filter, may be handed off or deferred
The key events feature is intentionally less detailed than a paid MedBrief chronology: it provides a lightweight AI-generated summary of key events as a quick overview
Accurately generating key events from records is difficult, and key events as an AI-generated output is still being refined
The new CER index will be interactive, supporting date-range filtering and keyword search over the extracted heading content
The CER index extracts bookmarks where they exist; if not, it uses a visual language model to extract headings visually
The CER index will launch exclusively on Epic, with other CER types attempted later
The CER index approach pivoted from attempting to identify clinical sections to extracting headings and bookmarks
Capturing ECA data on the platform stores it in the database, making it accessible to the AI team for downstream use such as MedBrief Match
The ECA wizard aggregates clinician answers to breach-of-duty and causation questions and generates a structured output
